Hi, it's Tony Robbins again. Welcome to Behind the Scenes, the story behind the story at Breakthrough. This is for you obsessed people again that want to go deeper and want to know more. Seriously, I wanted to share with you a little bit about why we picked this particular couple, what the story is really about. Tell you some of the strategies that you may not have seen on the air that we used to help turn them around because we only have 44 minutes nat after the commercials. And then most importantly, show you some things that you might want to use to change your own life moving forward and kind of tell you what a couple is today. And our theme here really was the death of fear. This is about, you know, blame, shame, and the real game of life. You know, we picked this couple because first of all, I was moved because I could see this man, Ron, was a really good man, but he was broken. And unfortunately, they represent so many people in our country today and that's why I thought they were so relatable. Here's a man who spent 23 years of his life dedicated to a company and he wasn't somebody that failed, he succeeded. He was so successful that, you know, he was given raises, but when the company had a downsize and when push came to shove and this economy crunched down like so many millions of other Americans, boom, suddenly he was redundant. All of a sudden there was no job. What I liked about Ron was the fact that he didn't give up. And if you watched the show, you know he went out and started his own business. You also know that he wasn't successful at that. Starting a business is not always easy and certainly in these times, you really have to have a competitive edge and Ron didn't quite have that in the particular area he went to work in. At the same time, when you see this family and you see this man losing his home, losing his life savings, losing his job, losing his sense of identity, and then the worst of all, losing his wife, losing that love and connection they once had and then maybe losing his children, I mean, I had to help. So the question is, what's this story really about though? On the surface, when we start out, it looks like it's a story of tragedy of a man who's had all these things happen to him. And it is true. Lots of things in our lives happen. Events occur that are outside of our control. Whether you're a fisherman down, you know, working in the Gulf States or whether you're somebody that gets laid off a job because the whole industry changes, it doesn't matter what it is outside of us. There are things that affect our livelihood, they affect our physical health, they affect our relationships, they affect our families. And so we can sit and feel sorry for ourselves or we do what most people do, which is find something to blame. And the problem with the blame game is the person that gets destroyed in that game, the person always loses, is the person who plays the game. As tough as it is, what we have to do is deal with the cards we're dealt. And what I saw here with this man who was working hard to deal with the cards he dealt, but now he is broken. Because for some reason, even though he'd been resilient after losing his business as well, he lost his confidence, he lost his edge. And he found himself in a position where it'd be very easy for you to see the beginning of the show and start saying the problem is his wife. Clearly Marie, she's unfair. She wants him to do everything. She's blaming him. He's tried. He's failed. She's blaming him for everything. She's mad and angry. She's going to get divorced and take his kids. She's being unreasonable. Why aren't they going to do this together? But if you do that, you'd be playing the blame game too. And most of us do. What you got to understand is when people are blaming what they really are is afraid. This was really to me about fear. This was really to me about showing people that you can't get over your fear till you walk through it and you can't walk through your fear. You can't face it while you're busy blaming some outside force. So in this man's case, I thought what I got to do is I got to get both of them to stop blaming because after blame comes shame. In this case, if you really listened and if you really watched Marie in the very beginning, she's blaming Ron and she's so angry. But really that anger underneath it comes from hurt and that hurt is coming from fear. And her big hurt that she talks about is she doesn't want to tell the kids. She doesn't want her neighbors to know they're losing their house. She's ashamed. When you're stuck into shame or blame, you can't make any progress. So the first step for me strategically was to make sure that I got both these people out of the place they ran. Ron is blaming himself. Then he's blaming his wife because she's not helping and she's not working. And in this game, everyone's being hurt. There's no progress. So here was the strategy. Let's get them out of their environment because right now they're living in this environment where they're being triggered. We brought them out to California. Let's put them in a neutral environment and let's get underneath and find out what's really going on with these two people. They're both convinced that their problems are purely financial, that if the financial problem wasn't there, their life would be perfect. Well, our lives might be perfect without problems, but they wouldn't be deep. They wouldn't be rich. We wouldn't grow as human beings. There'd be no sense of aliveness if everything was just static all the time. I tell people all the time, the metaphor I use is people say, you know, I don't want a life that's up and down like this. I want a life that's just straight across. You know, just I know how it is. I can count on it. Well, you know, go to a doctor's office and go get an EEG or an EKG and if it goes straight across like this, as I always tell people, you got a real problem. And that's what happens in our lives too. If we don't keep growing, life shows up to stimulate us to grow. And that's what's happened for this couple. So we brought them, as you probably saw, we brought them to Malibu and I had this first session with them and both these people were busy judging each other, blaming each other and feeling internal shame. And you'll see exactly how to turn them around, walking you through it step by step. So I think you'll see that progress. I mean, it starts out with crazy things where Ron is blaming her as much as she's blaming him and he's blaming her because she won't get a job because she won't be responsible like him. Like she won't help out. Of course, the more he blames her, the more she fights and doesn't want that job. And the more that happens, the more you end up in a situation where you have an ongoing war where there's no progress. So I kind of broke those patterns. The second piece though was once they start to respect each other a little bit, we need to build that respect. And when people think about stress, they think about stress from the perspective as something outside themselves, but stress is something physical. The mind does not know the difference between something you anticipate with fear stress and something that actually occurs. And so I thought, you know what, why don't they find a way to do something that shows them that stress can be exciting? Why don't I do something where I get them to have to work together and support each other? And so we look for something to be fun. This is a TV show also that would get people's attention. And so the first test, are they really committed, was they had a face of fear. And the fear they had a face was they had to get in a mid-jet and fly the speed of sound and take on 5Gs. You know, that process of taking on 5Gs, the physical stress in your body and managing that, not throwing up, not letting go, staying with it, was our first simple little task for them. But then we got to the real stuff. Now that I saw they're committed, they've made some progress in their communication. The big way that you kill fear, the only way you can kill fear, the death of fear is certain if you do what you're afraid of. I know that's overly simplistic, but the best truths in life are so damn simple. It's like I tell people all the time, common sense, this is all just common sense, but common sense is not too common. Most people, if you ever noticed, don't follow that common sense. They don't act on that common sense. People know what to do, but they don't do what they know. So I said, here's what we're going to do. What do they fear most? Losing their home, becoming homeless. Now I've had that experience, unfortunately, when I was 17 years old. My dad went back east that was on my own and the experience is not something I'd ever want to go through again, but it made me appreciate everything in my life from a different perspective and it made me strong. And so I said, here's a chance for us to do this. And we worked it out to go to the mission and we said, listen, we don't want these people taking away resources of other homeless people. Are you cool with this so we can put the, you know, the flashlight, the spotlight, so to speak, on what's happening down here? And the mission was supportive of it. And so we set up the process and we wanted to make sure that was real for them. So the cameras are only there for certain periods of time. Then we got away from them and they were just living homeless. That's what most people don't realize. And I need to put them in an environment where these two could rebuild trust where they had to stick together. They had the same interests protecting one another. And hopefully we're on with step up as a man in a way where Marie would really appreciate him again. And we could change that dynamic of their relationship. And I'm sure as you saw on the show, there was a shift in Marie, as she began to look around and see, my God, he's here for me no matter what. And that brought him together because that also brought along with the trust, it brought respect. And what is respect in any relationship? How do you know if you respect somebody? You trust them because you have things in common. You have the same interests. You're looking out for the same things, each other in this case. But the respect comes because you bring something to the table that your partner doesn't have. And in this case, Ron brought this vigilance that she didn't have. He brought this inner strength that in this situation she didn't have. She has plenty to offer him in other areas of life, but we need to change that dynamic where that respect and trust was back together. And so for days and days living on the street here, they got these lessons. I also wanted to get the lesson that you're always going to be worried about yourself. You're always going to be in fear as long as you're focused on yourself. And so they have the opportunity to come in and help inside, feed people, clean, do things of that nature, serve. And what they began to find is that life is really, really painful when all you do is sit around and think about your problems. But even if you're not doing something that's exciting, but you're doing something that's meaningful, or you're doing something to help, there's a reward in the effort. Most people want all the rewards without the effort, but effort is the reward. And they began to experience this. And they would live up most during that week. Again, we couldn't put it all on camera. And some of these little interactions that occurred where they were helping somebody out and they saw this light back in that person's eyes and they felt like we might be on the street, but our life really matters. We can make a difference and watching them make a difference for each other. What was beautiful is Marie, who was ready to quit the relationship now has fallen deep in love with her husband. He's feeling this strength that he can make a difference. He was the broken man. Remember? He was the guy who thought nothing he did worked. And now on the street where it really matters, he understood that he could really truly make that difference. So there's a bunch of clips that you can look at here, but I think the most important part of the show didn't make the show. That's just the truth. It's the most emotional part of the show. I think it's the most viable part of the show, but there's only so much you can do in 44 minutes and have a storyline. And that is this. After we got John and Marie and they got the lessons down there in Skid Row, we had interesting experiences down there where people were going to vote whether they stayed or left. And that was not staged. I told everybody there, it's your vote. If you think they got the lessons, stand up. If you all stand up, they go home. If even one person doesn't stand up, they stay here for another 10 days. There were some stress between Ron and Marie during that time, the stress within our crew about whether this was the right thing to do. And what it really did, it created a pressure on Ron and Marie to kick things over the edge and take what they've learned inside themselves. And they really did. But here's the part that didn't make the show. This is the story behind the story. And you'll be able to watch this clip. And I really encourage you to do this. I found out that Ron had not spoken to his parents in five years, either his mother or his father. And he used to be super close to them. And then all of a sudden I understood why Ron was such a broken man. See, it's one thing to lose your career and your income and your savings and maybe the business you start. But when you lose your wife and kids in your home, but you lose your wife and kids, now you're getting down to the core and Ron had nowhere else to go. He didn't have a mother or father or somebody else that he could connect with. And that's why he was broken. And I found out that there was a lot of upsets between the two of them. And I thought, how many families are there in this world where people have said something or done something or offended somebody in the family and then people just don't talk for years. It's unbelievably painful and destructive. So I told Ron and Marie, I was going to meet them back in New York at Marie's sister's house, which they both love. And I met him outside and I said, listen, you know, I really want to understand, you know, is there anybody else we're going to invite some of the family of there's anybody who's not here that, you know, we should invite. And they said, well, no, that, you know, my sister and a few people, that's it. And then I took them inside and there was Ron's parents standing there. And they, you know, were pretty emotional themselves. So you'll see the clip. I won't tell you all the details, but I'll tell you is there wasn't a dry member, I and then members of the crew in that room, because almost everybody has had somebody they really cared about where they felt some separation from them and watching this family heal and seeing again, the blame game, the blame game that we play, you know, you know, Marie and Ron's mom had had some conflict in their interpretations of each other. But the truth of the matter is they both, everybody loved everybody. It was just all these perceptions, all these blame and shame games that were going on. And we broke through them all and the transformation and Ron, it brought all his strength back because now his family was really healed. The rest of the show, I won't go through, you've seen the show, the elements that are there. I will just tell you that if you think that we just gave Maria a job, she went on 18 interviews, no one gave her a job. She got that job for real. And if you think that Ron got some wonderful job, we gave him an opportunity, but working for Willie Deagle, that guy's not exaggerating. The guy, if you ever go to that steakhouse, you'll see he is insane. He is completely, he did things that we knew if we showed it on the air, people would think it was a setup. He had Ron doing push-ups to show that he was really committed. He had Ron carrying, you know, a big piece of beef across Manhattan, literally running down the sidewalk to see if he would really gut it out. I mean, this guy, we didn't put in the show because you go, ah, that's all BS, you'd never do it. He does that to his employees. So Ron has done it. So where are they today? Well, you can click on and see a little clip from them. They just recently went to Unleash the Power Within seminar. We had a one-year reunion for all the families that went through the show here, all six families. And so you just hear a few moments. The biggest thing is not even what people say. It's look in Marie's eyes and see if that's the same woman that you saw in the beginning of that show. And Ron's eyes as well. I think you'll see the difference. But he got his job. He's working his tail off of the steakhouse. They're together in the relationship at a level like they never dreamed was possible before. They've made themselves, their family and their kids first. They've told the kids the truth and enrolled them. This is a winter time. It's not forever, but during the season, we've got to be smart. They still find a way to do well. They don't have enough money to pay off all their debts, but they're not losing their house anymore. And they're making progress like most Americans. So I'm really, really proud of them. The core of their relationship, which is the thing we wanted to change when they got reunited, when they stopped blaming, when they stopped shaming each other and they came together, that has not changed in a year. In fact, Marie was telling me the other day, she said, Ron is a better man for me. She says, I hope I am for him too, but he is a better man for me than he ever was in the beginning of our marriage. It's the best that's ever been. She said, I feel so supported by him. And Marie has a job, is taking care of the kids. And it's summertime right now, but when summer ends, she's going to get a second job so they have enough money and she's looking forward to it. She's excited about it.
